# Phase 0 - The Mathematical Arsenal

## Introduction

Imagine you have just arrived in Kathmandu from a small village, and you want to become an architect. You have a sharp mind, good instincts, and you can already eyeball distances reasonably well. Someone hands you a blueprint of a building and asks you to verify it. You squint at it, compare lengths by eye, and say "looks fine." Then the building gets constructed and the roof collapses — not because you were careless, but because eyeballing is not the same as measuring. You did not have the tools to catch the error. The problem was never your intelligence. It was your arsenal.

Phase 0 is the arsenal. Every concept you will encounter in this roadmap — morphing one probability distribution into another, building neural networks that respect the shape of a molecule, generating a protein backbone that obeys the laws of physics — ultimately rests on a precise, shared language of mathematics. Without that language, you can follow the steps but you cannot see why they work, and more importantly, you cannot see when they break.

Here is the honest picture of what Phase 0 is doing. Right now, when you think about mathematics, you likely think about calculation — plug in numbers, turn a crank, get an answer. What Phase 0 does is shift your thinking from calculation to structure. You stop asking "what is the answer?" and start asking "what kind of object is this, and what are the rules that govern it?" That shift is not cosmetic. It is the difference between a carpenter who can follow a plan and one who understands why wood splits along the grain.

The phase has five components, each building on the last. Real Analysis and Measure Theory give you a rigorous language for probability and infinity. Functional Analysis and Optimization give you the tools to reason about functions as objects you can stretch, compare, and minimize — because a neural network is ultimately just a very complicated function. Differential Geometry gives you the vocabulary to talk about curved spaces, because data does not live on a flat grid; it lives on surfaces and manifolds. Advanced Linear Algebra deepens what you already know from MATH 101 and MATH 103, revealing the geometric meaning behind operations you have been performing mechanically. And Python for Research gives you the computational laboratory where all of this becomes tangible.

The break moment for Phase 0 as a whole comes later — repeatedly — but its seed is here: the naive assumption is that the mathematics you already know is sufficient, that calculus and matrix multiplication will carry you through. That assumption breaks the first time you try to ask "what is the probability that a randomly chosen real number is rational?" and your existing tools give you no coherent answer. Measure Theory exists precisely because that question broke the naive framework. Every chapter in Phase 0 was born from a similar moment of breakage.

---
---
